title: Twenty-four envelopes
summary: A short essay about why a friend sent paper wedding invitations in 2024 — and what the slow act was really saying.
body:
Last summer my best friend got married. The wedding was quiet — twenty-five guests, a small church near her parents' farm, and lunch in the garden. Before the wedding, she sent paper invitations. She did not send a digital invitation. She did not text us.

Each invitation came in a small white envelope. My name was written by hand. The paper was thick. The information was short: the date, the time, the place, and one sentence: 'I would like you to be there.'

A digital invitation is fast. It is free. It can be sent to one hundred people in a second. A paper invitation is slow. It costs a few coins. It must be sent at least three weeks before the day.

The slowness is the point. A paper invitation says, 'I sat at a table. I wrote your name with a pen. I put it in an envelope. I walked to the post office. I bought a stamp. I dropped the envelope into the box. I did all of this for you. And I will do it again, twenty-four more times.'

Nobody needs to receive a paper invitation. Some people want to. The difference is small. It is also not nothing.

I still have the invitation. It is on my fridge.

grammarNotes.title: Past simple chain to show effort
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng chuỗi câu quá khứ đơn ngắn để miêu tả một loạt hành động — một cách hiệu quả để cho thấy 'công sức bỏ ra'. Trong bài: 'I sat at a table. I wrote your name with a pen. I put it in an envelope. I walked to the post office. I bought a stamp. I dropped the envelope into the box.' Mỗi câu là một động tác. Khi xếp cạnh nhau, chuỗi này tự nó là một lập luận: 'Nhìn xem có bao nhiêu bước.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 chains short past-simple sentences to dramatise effort. The essay: 'I sat at a table. I wrote your name with a pen. I put it in an envelope. I walked to the post office. I bought a stamp. I dropped the envelope into the box.' Each sentence is one action. The list itself is the argument: 'Look at how many small steps this was.'

translationVi:
Mùa hè năm ngoái bạn thân tôi cưới. Đám cưới yên — hai mươi lăm khách, một nhà thờ nhỏ gần trang trại của bố mẹ cô, và bữa trưa ngoài vườn. Trước hôm cưới, cô gửi thiệp mời bằng giấy. Cô không gửi thiệp điện tử. Cô không nhắn tin cho chúng tôi.

Mỗi tấm thiệp đến trong một phong bì trắng nhỏ. Tên tôi viết tay. Giấy dày. Nội dung ngắn: ngày, giờ, địa điểm, và một câu: 'Mình mong có bạn ở đó.'

Thiệp điện tử nhanh. Miễn phí. Có thể gửi cho một trăm người trong một giây. Thiệp giấy chậm. Tốn vài đồng. Phải gửi trước đó ít nhất ba tuần.

Sự chậm chính là cốt lõi. Một tấm thiệp giấy nói: 'Mình ngồi vào bàn. Mình viết tên bạn bằng bút. Mình bỏ vào phong bì. Mình đi bộ ra bưu điện. Mình mua tem. Mình thả phong bì vào hộp. Mình làm tất cả những điều đó cho bạn. Và mình sẽ làm lại hai mươi tư lần nữa.'

Không ai cần phải nhận thiệp giấy. Một số người muốn. Khác biệt nhỏ. Cũng không phải không có gì.

Tôi vẫn giữ tấm thiệp. Nó dán trên tủ lạnh.

questions.prompt: How many guests were at the wedding?

options: Paper invitations in small white envelopes; not digital, not text

questions.prompt: What was written on the invitation?
options: Date, time, place, and one sentence: 'I would like you to be there.'

questions.prompt: How long before the day must a paper invitation be sent?
questions.explanation: 'It must be sent at least three weeks before the day.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say a paper invitation 'says'?
options: It lists the small steps the sender took — sitting, writing, walking, buying a stamp, dropping the envelope — and that the sender will do it again twenty-four more times.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's main reason for praising paper invitations?
options: The slowness is the point — it shows time spent for each guest.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say about 'need' vs. 'want'?
options: Nobody needs to receive one; some people want to. The difference is small but not nothing.

questions.prompt: What is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That digital invitations should be banned.
That a paper invitation costs more time than a digital one.
That nobody literally needs a paper invitation.
That a slow act can still mean something.
questions.explanation: The writer accepts that paper is not necessary; she only defends what it does.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the essay?
options: Choosing the slow way on purpose can communicate something about each recipient that the fast way cannot.
questions.explanation: The essay defends the visible effort behind paper invitations.
questions.hint: Look for 'the slowness is the point.'

cloze.template:
Last summer my best friend got {{b1}}. The wedding was quiet — twenty-five guests, a small church near her parents' farm, and lunch in the garden. Before the wedding, she sent paper {{b2}}. She did not send a digital invitation. She did not text us.

Each invitation came in a small white {{b3}}. My name was written by hand. The paper was thick. The information was short: the date, the time, the place, and one sentence: 'I would like you to be there.'

A digital invitation is fast. It is {{b4}}. It can be sent to one hundred people in a second. A paper invitation is slow. It costs a few coins. It must be sent at least {{b5}} weeks before the day.

The slowness is the {{b6}}. A paper invitation says, 'I sat at a table. I wrote your name with a {{b7}}. I put it in an envelope. I walked to the post office. I bought a {{b8}}. I dropped the envelope into the box. I did all of this for you. And I will do it again, twenty-four more times.'

Nobody needs to receive a paper invitation. Some people want to. The difference is small. It is also not {{b9}}.

I still have the invitation. It is on my {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer says her friend's paper invitation 'said' a list of small steps. Is there a place in your life where you have replaced a slow act with a fast one — and lost the small steps that were the message? What would happen if you brought the slow version back for one specific person?
